1:
The chapter opens on a three-year famine, and the witnesses divide first over when it fell and then over why God’s stroke reached so far back. On the chronology the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and the reformed Gill side with the older interpreters that these events belong long before Absalom’s rebellion, however late they stand in the book. Wesley reads the vague then—explained as “in the days of David”—as the writer’s own hint that he gathers matters by subject rather than sequence, and lists his grounds: that Saul’s crime should not lie unpunished so long, that the bones of Saul and Jonathan waited so long for burial, and that David in old age would hardly have fought a giant (v.15). Ellicott, for the grammatical-historical school, calls the phrase “purposely indefinite,” notes the story’s absence from Chronicles, and finds in the unusual expression “sought the face of the LORD” a sign that the compiler drew this narrative from a separate record.

The heart of the verse is the divine answer, “It is for Saul, and for his bloody house.” Gill recalls that no such massacre of the Gibeonites appears in Saul’s own history, and Clarke presses the silence harder still—“no such fact is mentioned in the life and transactions of Saul; nor is there any reference to it in any other part of Scripture.” Matthew Henry, the puritan-evangelical voice, supplies the moral weight: Saul, “under colour of zeal for the honour of Israel,” had sought to root out a people whose lives Joshua had secured by the public faith (Josh. 9); “that which made this an exceedingly sinful sin was that he not only shed innocent blood, but therein violated the solemn oath,” and so “see what brought ruin on Saul’s house: it was a bloody house.”

The difficulty the witnesses feel most is theodicy—why the delay, and why the land and Saul’s sons should smart for a dead king’s crime. Henry answers that “God’s judgments often look a great way back,” that “time does not wear out the guilt of sin,” and that “there is no statute of limitation to be pleaded against God’s demands.” Wesley meets the objection head-on: God did punish Saul in his lifetime; He may justly visit temporal penalties on a man’s posterity when He pleases; and the people were likely not innocent, having advised, encouraged, or abetted the crime. Henry adds the pastoral note that David himself was slow, not consulting the oracle till the third year—“even good men are often slack and remiss in doing their duty”—yet when he did seek God, “God was ready in his answer.” Beneath the debate the witnesses land together on Henry’s counsel: under God’s judgments we ought to “enquire into the grounds of the controversy,” saying with Job, “show me wherefore thou contendest with me” (Job 10:2).
2:
The narrator pauses to identify the injured party, and the witnesses use the parenthesis to weigh Saul’s guilt and Israel’s. On the ethnography Clarke and Keil and Delitzsch note the apparent discrepancy—the Gibeonites were Hivites (Josh. 9:7), yet here “Amorites”; both resolve it the same way, that Amorites is a general name for the Canaanite peoples, as at Gen. 15:16 and Amos 2:9. Ellicott adds, from v.8, that the Gibeonites “had never become incorporated with the Israelites by circumcision, but remained a distinct people,” which is why the breach of faith against them was so grave.

On Saul’s motive the wesleyan-arminian Wesley reads the single word “sought” as cruelty dressed in the colour of justice: Saul “sought how he might cut them off with some colour of justice,” aggravating their faults, “oppressing them with excessive labours, and intending by degrees to wear them out.” Ellicott, for the grammatical-historical school, sees the same hypocrisy—Saul’s “zeal” was “of the same ungodly character with many other acts of his reign, in which pride, arrogance, and self-will were cloaked under a zeal for God’s honour and His people’s welfare.”

But the verse’s deepest problem, as Ellicott frames it, is the pair of questions every reader asks: why was the punishment so long delayed, and why did it fall on David and a later generation who had no share in the sin? His answer is the doctrine of national solidarity: “Israel both sinned and was punished as a nation.” Saul slew the Gibeonites “not simply as the son of Kish, but as the king of Israel,” and so “involved all Israel with him in the violation of the national oath”; until the evil was purged, “all Israel must suffer.” The delay itself was instructive, for “the lesson of the continuity of the nation’s life, and of its continued responsibility from age to age, was greatly enhanced by the delay.” Ellicott offers a second reason for the deferral—Saul had “so many other grievous sins” to answer for that “it was hardly possible to bring out during his lifetime the special Divine displeasure at this one.” The reformed Gill contributes the plain frame, that David “sent messengers” and summoned them, while Keil and Delitzsch underline that the divine word of v.1 had indicated only the general cause, so David must now go to the sufferers themselves to learn the wrong. The witnesses converge on Ellicott’s principle: a nation’s oath binds a nation, and its guilt, unexpiated, descends.
3:
David’s question—“What shall I do for you? and wherewith shall I make the atonement, that ye may bless the inheritance of the LORD?”—draws the witnesses to the word atonement and to a sharp objection from Clarke. The grammatical-historical Ellicott dwells on the technical term: it is “the same technical word as is used throughout the Law in connection with the propitiatory sacrifices,” meaning literally “to cover up,” and David asks how he may so “cover up the sin of Saul as to remove it from the sight of those against whom it had been committed—the Gibeonites as the earthly sufferers from it, and God Himself as the one against whom he had chiefly offended.” Only then, Ellicott adds, “might God’s blessing again return to His people,” which is the force of David’s hope that they will “bless the inheritance of the LORD.”

The wesleyan-arminian Clarke is troubled by the whole procedure: “It is very strange that a choice of this kind should be left to such a people. Why not ask this of God himself?” His unease—that a heathen remnant should be handed the terms of expiation—will grow into his running protest across the chapter, but here it stands as one pointed question. The reformed Gill and Keil and Delitzsch keep to the sense of the words: Gill glosses the king’s aim simply, “by way of satisfaction for the injuries done them,” and Keil and Delitzsch read the opening of the negotiation, in which David inquires “wherewith shall I make the atonement, that ye may bless the inheritance of the LORD,” expecting the Gibeonites to name a price.

Beneath the disagreement the witnesses agree on what the verse teaches about guilt and reconciliation. David does not treat the famine as a natural misfortune to be waited out; he seeks a real covering for a real crime, and he binds the removal of God’s anger to the blessing of the wronged. As Henry said of the case at large, “in vain do we expect mercy from God, unless we do justice upon our sins,” and the expense of “a just restitution is well bestowed for the retrieving of the blessing of those that were ready to perish.” The atonement David seeks looks forward, in the witnesses’ reading, to the true covering of guilt, though here it is sought at a grievous human cost that Clarke will not let the reader forget. What David will not do is leave the wrong uncovered; better a hard reckoning sought in earnest than a blessing presumed while innocent blood cries from the ground.
4:
The Gibeonites’ reply refuses money and disclaims any wider vengeance, and the witnesses read both refusals as marks of a strangely lawful restraint. On the first, Ellicott, for the grammatical-historical school, observes that “money compensations for sins of blood were extremely common among all ancient nations, but were expressly forbidden in the Law of Moses” (Num. 35:31); “in this respect the Gibeonites appear to have accepted the teaching of the law of Israel.” The people whom Saul tried to exterminate prove more careful of Moses’ law than the king who claimed to act in zeal for it.

On the second refusal—“neither for us shalt thou kill any man in Israel”—Ellicott notes the fairness of their view: though the guilt of Saul’s sin, until it should be expiated, “rested upon all Israel,” the Gibeonites “recognise that it had been committed by him, and do not seek that, apart from their connection with him, any Israelite should suffer on their account.” They will not have a random Israelite die; they ask only from the house of the offender. Ellicott adds that “David appreciates the fairness of their view of the matter, and promises beforehand to do whatever they shall require”—the blank warrant Henry described, “What you shall say, that will I do.”

The reformed Gill marks only the turn of the dialogue, “in reply to his motion,” letting the Gibeonites’ words carry their own weight. Matthew Henry, the puritan-evangelical voice, draws the lesson the refusals invite: “money is no satisfaction for blood,” for “it is the ancient law that blood calls for blood” (Gen. 9:6), and “those over-value money and under-value life, that sell the blood of their relations for corruptible things, such as silver and gold.” Henry adds that the Gibeonites, though they might have demanded release from their servitude by the equity of the law that frees a servant for a lost eye (Exod. 21:26), “did not insist on this,” for “though the covenant was broken on the other side, it should not be broken on theirs.” They were the Nethinim, “given to God and his people Israel,” and “would not seem weary of the service.” The witnesses find in the wronged people a restraint that shames both Saul and every reader tempted to price a life.
5:
Now the Gibeonites name the man and the crime, and the witnesses hear in their phrase—“the man that consumed us, and that devised against us that we should be destroyed from remaining in any of the coasts of Israel”—the language of attempted extermination. The reformed Gill unfolds the verb: Saul “lessened their number by cruel oppressions of some, and by taking away the lives of others,” so that “consumed” covers both the grinding servitude and the outright slaughter. The charge is not merely of murders done but of a settled design “devised against” them to blot them from the land altogether.

Keil and Delitzsch, for the grammatical-historical school, treat these verses together and supply the legal ground on which David could grant so grave a request. He “granted the request, because, according to the law in Num. 35:33, blood-guiltiness when resting upon the land could only be expiated by the blood of the criminal.” This is the principle that governs the whole transaction: innocent blood shed and unavenged defiles the land, and the land’s defilement, not private revenge, is what the executions are meant to purge. Keil and Delitzsch go on to name the persons David would give up and the one he would spare—Mephibosheth kept back “for the sake of the bond of friendship which he had formed with Jonathan on oath” (1 Sam. 18:3; 20:16), and the two sons of Rizpah and five sons of Merab delivered over—reading forward into the verses that follow.

Beneath the grim demand the witnesses keep the verse’s own accent: it is Saul who is named, Saul who “devised,” and it is the destruction he plotted against a defenceless people that now recoils upon his house. The Gibeonites, who had endured in silence for years, at last put the crime into words, and their very restraint in charging only “the man that consumed us”—not all Israel, not David—keeps their demand within the bounds of justice rather than revenge. As the witnesses read it, a sin that “devised” a people’s ruin does not vanish with the schemer’s death; it waits, as Henry said, to be “reckoned for,” and when the reckoning comes it falls exactly where the guilt lay—on the man who consumed them and the house that shared his spirit.
6:
The Gibeonites’ precise demand—seven sons of Saul, to be hanged “unto the LORD in Gibeah of Saul”—raises the sharpest questions of the chapter, and the witnesses answer them very differently. On the number seven, Ellicott, for the grammatical-historical school, sees it “fixed upon as being first, a considerable and sufficient number,” and “on account of its sacred associations, and as the representative of completeness.” The reformed Gill offers two guesses—that seven Gibeonites in particular had been slain, or, more likely, that they knew there were “no more besides Mephibosheth” whom David would wish to spare.

A first debate concerns guilt. The wesleyan-arminian Clarke will not grant that the seven deserved to die: “it is supposed that the persons chosen were principal in assisting Saul to exterminate the Gibeonites. But where is the proof of this?” Ellicott meets him with the ancient doctrine of the house: “the head of the house and his household were closely identified in all the ideas of antiquity,” and Saul being dead, “his male descendants were considered as standing in his place, representing him, and responsible for his acts.” A second and hotter debate concerns the phrase “unto the LORD”—was this a human sacrifice? Ellicott flatly denies it: the words mean “publicly” (comparing Num. 25:4), for “the sin had been outrageous; its punishment must be conspicuous,” and “the idea of regarding the execution of these men as a propitiatory human sacrifice is utterly destitute of any shadow of support.” A third puzzle is the title “whom the LORD did choose”; Ellicott ties it to the same “unto the LORD,” for what Saul had done he had done “as the head of the theocracy, as God’s chosen ruler, and now his family must be punished in the presence of Him against whom he had offended.”

The decisive question—whether David sinned in granting this—the wesleyan-arminian Wesley answers by appeal to divine direction. David acted “having doubtless consulted God,” who “commanded that justice should be done”; the proof is “that God was well pleased with the action, which he would not have been, if David had done it without his command,” for otherwise it had been “contrary to a double law of God” (Deut. 24:16). So the witnesses divide: Clarke doubts the justice of it, while Ellicott and Wesley defend it as public, representative, and divinely sanctioned—yet all agree the verse means to turn Saul’s hidden crime into a conspicuous reckoning, staged at his own door.
7:
The single act of mercy in a grim scene is David’s sparing of Mephibosheth, and the witnesses dwell on the oath that governed his choice. Because the Gibeonites had asked only for a number, not for named men, the selection lay with David; the reformed Gill and the wesleyan-arminian Wesley both stress that “it was at David’s choice whom to spare,” and Gill notes the careful description of “Mephibosheth, the son of Jonathan, the son of Saul”—added, as Wesley says, “to distinguish him from the other Mephibosheth” of v.8, one of the sons of Rizpah who would be given up.

Keil and Delitzsch, for the grammatical-historical school, name the ground of the sparing: David acted “because of the LORD’s oath that was between them, between David and Jonathan,” the covenant of friendship sworn long before (1 Sam. 18:3; 20:16). Here the witnesses catch a moral symmetry that Henry drew out on the chapter: David is engaged in avenging one broken oath—Saul’s violated league with the Gibeonites—and takes the greatest care not to break another in the doing of it, so that “while he was avenging the breach of one oath, he might not himself break another.”

The choice also answers, in advance, the suspicious reader. Ellicott and others note across these verses that if David had wished to use the Gibeonites’ demand to secure his own throne, he would have surrendered exactly the man he protects; instead he shields “the son of Jonathan,” the one descendant of Saul in the male line whose claim might have troubled him, and gives up sons and grandsons who posed no such threat. So the sparing of Mephibosheth becomes the proof that David acted not from policy or grudge but under the constraint of justice and of a sworn word. The witnesses land where the verse does: an oath, once truly sworn before the LORD, holds even when it is inconvenient—and the king who honours his own covenant is the fit instrument to punish the king who broke his. Mercy and judgment meet in the one act; the same day that surrenders seven to death shelters one for a promise’s sake.
8:
This verse names the seven and raises the chapter’s most discussed textual crux—the “five sons of Michal … whom she brought up for Adriel.” The witnesses agree the name is a copyist’s slip. The wesleyan-arminian Clarke lays it out: “Michal, Saul’s daughter, was never married to Adriel, but to David, and afterwards to Phaltiel”; from 1 Sam. 18:19 we learn it was Merab who was given to Adriel, and Clarke reports that two of Kennicott’s manuscripts read “Merab,” while the Chaldee renders it “the five sons of Merab which Michal the daughter of Saul brought up”—a reading he says “cuts the knot.” Ellicott, for the grammatical-historical school, reaches the same verdict: “the text has Michal instead of Merab; but this must be an error of the scribe,” since Merab married Adriel and “Michal was childless” (2 Sam. 6:23). Both note that the English “brought up for” follows the Chaldee, the bare Hebrew reading simply “to.”

Behind the names the witnesses see a defence of David’s integrity. Ellicott takes up the cynical suggestion “that David took advantage of this opportunity to strengthen himself further against the house of Saul” and sets it aside on two grounds: first, David “could not lawfully refuse the demand of the Gibeonites, since the Law absolutely required that blood-guiltiness should be expiated by the blood of the offender” (Num. 35:33); and second, “David’s choice of victims was directly opposed to such a supposition,” for he spared “the only descendants of Saul in the male line, who only could have advanced any claim to the throne,” and gave up the two sons of Rizpah—the concubine “with whom Abner had committed adultery” (2 Sam. 3:7)—and the sons of a daughter.

Matthew Henry, the puritan-evangelical voice, hears in the mention of Merab an older thread of retribution: Saul had promised her to David and then given her to Adriel “with a design to provoke him,” and now that treachery too is quietly reckoned. He borrows Bishop Hall’s warning: “it is a dangerous matter to offer injury to any of God’s faithful ones; if their meekness have easily remitted it, their God will not pass it over without a severe retribution, though it may be long first.” The reformed Gill simply identifies Rizpah as “Saul’s concubine.” So the witnesses hold the verse steady on two points: the scribe wrote Michal for Merab, and David’s very selection clears him of the charge of using justice as a cover for ambition.
9:
The execution is carried out “in the days of harvest … in the beginning of barley harvest,” and the witnesses fix on the season, for it sets the clock for all that follows. The wesleyan-arminian Clarke dates it precisely: barley harvest “happened in Judea about the vernal equinox, or the 21st of March.” Ellicott, for the grammatical-historical school, places it “immediately after the Passover” (Lev. 23:10), “and therefore about the middle of April,” and reckons the consequence: since “the rains of autumn began in October,” the watch of Rizpah that opens in the next verse “must have been about six months.” He explains, too, why the bodies hung unburied so long—“it was not until these rains began … that the people were assured of the Divine forgiveness, and therefore the bodies of the executed were left unburied until then.”

The reformed Gill keeps the tally of persons straight, noting that David “delivered them into the hands of the Gibeonites”—“the two sons of Rizpah and the five sons of Merab, two sons of Saul and five grandsons.” The count of seven is complete; the deed is done not by David’s own hand but by the wronged people’s, as they had asked. They “fell all seven together,” the text says, a single act of judgment closing the account of Saul’s bloody house.

Matthew Henry, the puritan-evangelical voice, gathers the theological meaning of the harvest setting: the seven were “put to death in the days of harvest … to show that they were thus sacrificed for the turning away of that wrath of God which had withheld from them their harvest-mercies for some years past, and to obtain his favour in the present harvest.” The timing preaches. A land that had seen three barren years now sheds this blood at the very hinge of the grain-year, pleading for the rain. Henry draws the edge sharp—“there is no way of appeasing God’s anger but by mortifying and crucifying our lusts and corruptions; in vain do we expect mercy from God, unless we do justice upon our sins.” The witnesses read the season, then, not as incidental but as the whole point: at harvest the guilt of blood is purged so that the harvest may return.
10:
Rizpah’s vigil over her dead is the emotional summit of the chapter, and the witnesses respond with unusual tenderness and unusual protest. The puritan-evangelical Henry reads her sackcloth spread upon the rock as at once excessive grief and noble love. On the one hand she “indulged her grief, as mourners are too apt to do, to no good purpose”; on the other she “testified her love,” letting the world know “that her sons died, not for any sin of their own … but for their father’s sin, and therefore her mind could not be alienated from them by their hard fate.” He marks her patient submission: she “neither steals nor forces away their dead bodies, though the divine law might have been cited to bear her out,” but keeps her lonely watch, protecting them from the birds by day and the beasts by night, lest they suffer the fate the scornful son is threatened with in Prov. 30:17.

The wesleyan-arminian Clarke can hardly bear the scene, and it provokes his fullest protest: “Who can read the account of Rizpah’s maternal affection for her sons … without feeling his mind deeply impressed with sorrows?” He turns the pathos into an argument against the whole transaction: “Did God require this sacrifice of Saul’s sons, probably all innocent of the alleged crime of their father? … Certainly not; God will not have man’s blood for sacrifice, no more than he will have swine’s blood.” The famine, he insists, “might have been removed, and the land properly purged, by offering the sacrifices prescribed by the law, and by a general humiliation of the people.” Where Ellicott and Wesley defended the execution, Clarke stands as the chapter’s dissenting conscience.

A quieter debate concerns “until water dropped upon them out of heaven.” Clarke argues firmly for “the time of the autumnal rains, which in that country commence about October,” reasoning that the whole setting demands a fixed periodical rain, not a casual shower, and marvels that “this poor broken-hearted woman could have endured … watching these bodies for more than five months.” Henry allows the rain came “perhaps … not many days after they were hung up,” reading it as the sign “that God was entreated for the land.” The reformed Gill and Wesley note the plain sense of the sackcloth—spread “as a tent to dwell in,” a rough shelter for the long watch—and Wesley repeats her silent testimony that “her sons died not for any sin of their own … but for their father’s sin.” For all their difference, the witnesses converge on Henry’s point: “when justice is done on earth, vengeance from heaven ceases”; and each of them, even the protesting Clarke, is held fast by the image of a mother who would not let the ravens have her sons.
11:
The news of Rizpah’s devotion reaches the king, and the witnesses agree it is the hinge that turns David from executioner to honourer of the dead. The reformed Gill weighs the report’s motive—“whether this was told out of good will or ill will is not certain”—but is sure of its effect: it “served to move pity and compassion in him to the woman, and to stir him up to give an honourable interment to Saul and his sons.” Gill sees a further providence in it: the burial “would show that this fact was not done out of personal pique and revenge to his family, but in obedience to the will of God, and the honour of his name.”

Keil and Delitzsch, for the grammatical-historical school, read the verse as the narrative bridge to the reburial: “when this touching care of Rizpah for the dead was told to David, he took care that the bones of the whole of the fallen royal house should be buried in the burial-place of Saul’s family.” The wesleyan-arminian Wesley catches the moral contagion of her example—David “heard it with so much approbation, that he thought fit to imitate her piety, being by her example provoked to do what hitherto he had neglected,” to bestow “an honourable interment on the remains of Saul and Jonathan, and, with them, upon those that are now put to death, that the honour done to them herein might be some comfort to this disconsolate widow.”

The witnesses thus find in one woman’s fidelity both a rebuke and a summons to the king. Her long vigil shames the neglect that had left Saul and Jonathan in a borrowed grave and the seven unburied, and it draws from David an act of public honour. What a mother’s grief begins, a king’s piety completes; and the whole is so ordered, Gill notes, that no onlooker could mistake David’s justice for private revenge. The verse is brief—a mere report told to David—but the witnesses see in it how God uses the tenderness of the lowly to move the conscience of the great, and how an act of love beside a scaffold can turn the current of a kingdom’s mourning toward mercy.
12:
David goes to gather the bones of Saul and Jonathan, and the witnesses use the verse to reconcile a small discrepancy and to admire a large magnanimity. The grammatical-historical Ellicott states the motive plainly: “moved by the story of Rizpah’s tender care, and wishing to show that he cherished no enmity against the house of Saul, David buried honourably the remains of Saul and of his descendants.” The reformed Gill recalls the distance—“fifty-two miles from Jerusalem,” by one reckoning—and suggests David acted “by his messengers” rather than in person.

The apparent contradiction is that 1 Sam. 31:10 says the Philistines fastened Saul’s body “to the wall of Beth-shan,” while here the men of Jabesh took it “from the street.” Ellicott harmonizes them: “the exact place where the Philistines hung up to public view the body of the slain and defeated monarch was the broad space or square, just inside the gate, where the people were wont to gather; and it was from the same place that they were taken.” Keil and Delitzsch reach the same result by philology, glossing the verb as “to take away secretly” and explaining that the market-place of an eastern town “is not in the middle of the town, but is an open place … against or in” the wall. Clarke adds the physical history—the men of Jabesh had “burned the bodies of Saul and his sons, and buried the remaining bones under a tree at Jabesh” (1 Sam. 31:12), which David “might have digged up again.”

Keil and Delitzsch also mark that this heroic-burial notice opens the seam to the war-records that follow, “probably taken from a history of David’s wars.” Ellicott preserves a curious textual relic: most Septuagint manuscripts add that “Dan the son of Joa, of the descendants of the giant, took them down.” The witnesses let the small puzzles resolve and keep their eye on the deed: the king once hunted by Saul now travels to reclaim Saul’s dishonoured dust. As Henry said, “though the shield of Saul was vilely cast away … yet let not royal dust be lost in the graves of the common people,” for humanity itself “obliges us to respect human bodies, especially of the great and good, in consideration both of what they have been and what they are to be.”
13:
This brief verse records the ingathering—David “brought up from thence the bones of Saul, and the bones of Jonathan his son,” and “they gathered the bones of them that were hanged.” With little to expound, the witnesses attend to the single word bones and to what it implies about the state of the remains. The reformed Gill notes only that these had “been buried there under an oak,” recalling the tree at Jabesh where the men of that city had laid them. The wesleyan-arminian Wesley supplies the grim detail the narrative assumes: they are called bones “having first burnt off the flesh which remained upon them when they were taken down,” and he points the reader back to the account of the men of Jabesh (1 Sam. 31:12).

Gill’s small note—that the bones had lain “under an oak”—reaches back to the loyal men of Jabesh-gilead, who at the peril of their lives had rescued the bodies from the Philistines’ wall and given them what burial they could; their earlier piety and David’s present piety meet over the same relics. The king does not disdain to reclaim even dust long dead and dishonoured.

The verse quietly joins two companies of the dead—the long-dead king and prince recovered from Gilboa’s aftermath, and the newly executed seven taken down from the hill of Gibeah. What had been scattered by defeat and by judgment is now gathered into one act of reverent collection. The witnesses see in the pairing a deliberate equalizing: Saul the anointed and the seven who died under a curse are handled with the same care, “gathered” together for one honourable grave. The distinction that hanging had made—that “he that is hanged is accursed of God”—is now overtaken, for, as Henry observed, “when God’s anger was turned away, they were no longer to be looked upon as a curse.” Thus the mere logistics of gathering bones carry the chapter’s turn from wrath to peace: the same hands that delivered the seven to death now tenderly collect their bones, and the same king who avenged the Gibeonites honours the house of the man who wronged them.
14:
The bones find their rest “in the country of Benjamin in Zelah, in the sepulchre of Kish his father,” and the chapter’s first half closes with the words “after that God was intreated for the land.” The witnesses take up the place, the family tomb, and a fine point of timing. On the place, Ellicott, for the grammatical-historical school, identifies Zelah as “according to Joshua 18:28 a town of Benjamin,” and though “it has not been identified,” reckons it “probably near Gibeah”—Saul’s own country, a fitting ground for the family grave in “the sepulchre of Kish his father.” The reformed Gill simply gathers the burial party, noting that with Saul and Jonathan were laid “those who had been hanged,” the two houses of the dead now sharing one tomb.

The theological point the witnesses press is the phrase “after that.” The wesleyan-arminian Wesley insists on its exact reference: “after those things were done which were before related; that is, after they were hanged up: for by that God was pacified, and not by their burial.” The expiation, in his reading, was accomplished by the execution that satisfied the broken oath, not by the honourable interment that followed; the burial is David’s act of reverence, not the atoning cause. Henry reasoned the same way about the rain, that the bodies were taken down “when it appeared that God was entreated for the land,” so that “when justice is done on earth, vengeance from heaven ceases.”

So the section ends where it began, with the land. A three-year famine had asked its question; the wrong of a dead king has been purged by the blood of his house and sealed by the rain from heaven; and the record can say at last that “God was intreated.” The witnesses read the whole episode as a hard lesson in national accountability and in the seriousness of a sworn word—yet one that closes, mercifully, with a grave, a rain, and a God who hears. And all this, Gill and Wesley note, the servants did faithfully, for “they performed all that the king commanded” before the blessing returned.
15:
The chapter’s second half turns to the giant-wars, and the witnesses read the opening notice—“the Philistines had yet war again with Israel: and David went down … and waxed faint”—as a study in aging valour and divine rescue. The puritan-evangelical Henry makes David’s weariness his theme. Though the Philistines could no longer field great numbers, “as long as they had any giants among them … they would never be quiet”; and David, “though old, desired not a writ of ease from the public service,” but “went down in person,” which Henry crowns with the tag Senescit, non segnescit—“he grows old, but not indolent.” Yet the old king “found himself deceived; age had cut his hair, and, after a little toil, he waxed faint. His body could not keep pace with his mind.” The champion, seeing his advantage, “thought to slay David; but God was not in his thoughts, and therefore in that very day they all perished.”

The grammatical-historical voices set the scene in time and source. Ellicott dates the war only loosely—“after David had become king of all Israel, and probably after he had become somewhat advanced in years”—and observes that 1 Chron. 20:4 places “much the same paragraph” right after the Ammonite war, “a mere juxta-position rather than … a chronological sequence.” Keil and Delitzsch judge the whole passage drawn “from a history of David’s wars drawn up in the form of chronicles,” introduced here “as practical proofs of the gracious deliverance” for which the psalm of the next chapter gives thanks. The wesleyan-arminian Clarke notes the narrative’s independence—“there is no mention of this war in the parallel place,” and “David waxed faint … is nowhere else mentioned”—while the reformed Gill suggests the Philistines were emboldened “by the number of giants among them, and … by the decline of David’s life.”

From the near-disaster Henry draws the pastoral heart of the verse. Abishai’s timely rescue in v.17 he credits above all to “the good providence of God, which brought him in to David’s succour in the moment of his extremity”; and he turns the scene into an allegory of the tempted saint: “in spiritual conflicts, even strong saints sometimes wax faint; then Satan attacks them furiously; but those that stand their ground and resist him shall be relieved, and made more than conquerors.” He notes that David “fainted, but he did not flee,” bravely keeping his ground till God sent help, as “Christ, in his agonies, was strengthened by an angel” (Luke 22:43). The witnesses hold together the plain history—an aging warrior overmatched and rescued—and its comfort: the light of Israel was in danger, yet “there is no strength, nor counsel, nor confidence against the LORD.”
16:
The first giant is named—Ishbi-benob, “of the sons of the giant,” with a spear of three hundred shekels of brass and “a new sword,” who “thought to have slain David.” The witnesses puzzle over the name, the weapon, and the danger. On the name, the grammatical-historical Ellicott confesses it “a strange one,” “generally thought that some error has crept into the text,” and floats the Speaker’s Commentary emendation by which the words become “they halted at Gob. And there was a man which was of the sons,” etc.—reading “Ishbi” as “they halted” and “benob” as “at Gob,” a place the following verses seem to require. Keil and Delitzsch prefer to keep it as a proper name meaning “his dwelling is on the height,” perhaps given “because of his inaccessible castle,” and identify him as a descendant of Raphah, “the tribe-father of the Rephaim, an ancient tribe of gigantic stature” of whom only “a few families were left even in Moses’ time” (Deut. 2:11).

On the weapon, Ellicott notes the spear-head weighed “about eight pounds; just half the weight of Goliath’s spear-head” (1 Sam. 17:7)—a deliberate echo, as if Israel now faced a lesser Goliath. The phrase “girded with a new sword” troubles both Ellicott and the wesleyan-arminian Clarke, since “the word sword is not in the original.” Clarke suggests it means the giant “had got new arms, a new coat of mail … or a strong or sharp sword”; Ellicott agrees it should be either “new armour” or “some otherwise unknown weapon.”

Keil and Delitzsch draw out the gravity of the moment for the king. David “was exhausted in this fight, and a Philistian giant thought to slay him; but Abishai came to his help and slew the giant.” The reformed Gill fixes only the pedigree—“of the sons of the giant … of the race of them.” The witnesses, for all their textual caution, agree on the scene the verse paints: a spent David, a fresh-armed monster half the bulk of the old champion of Gath but large enough to kill a weary man, and a rescue that will come only from another’s hand. The very uncertainty of the name and the sword becomes, in their treatment, a mark of the passage’s antiquity—a splinter of an old war-chronicle preserved because in it the light of Israel came within a spear’s length of going out.
17:
Abishai’s rescue and the soldiers’ oath give the verse both its drama and its most beloved phrase. The reformed Gill marks the swiftness of the deliverance—Abishai, “observing him in danger, made haste to his relief.” The grammatical-historical Ellicott raises a point of grammar over the killing: “the original leaves it doubtful whether Abishai is the nominative to the verb, or whether it should be simply he, referring to David,” and he reads v.22 as implying “that one at least of the sons of the giant fell by David’s own hand.” Henry resolved the ambiguity the same way, taking it that after Abishai “gave him a cordial … to relieve his fainting spirits, or appeared as his second,” it was David who “smote the Philistine and killed him.”

The oath that follows—“Thou shalt go no more out with us to battle, that thou quench not the light of Israel”—draws the witnesses’ warmest comment. Clarke, the wesleyan-arminian, lingers on the image: David “is here considered as the lamp by which all Israel was guided, and without whom all the nation must be involved in darkness”; “the lamp is the emblem of direction and support.” He even reaches for Homer, quoting the Iliad where Achilles laments that he was no “light” to Patroclus and his companions, to show how ancient and natural is the metaphor of the leader as his people’s light. Ellicott finds a chronological hint in the very title: that David “should be spoken of as ‘the light of Israel’ implies that his government over all Israel had continued long enough already to make its immense benefits sensible,” and he calls the oath “a solemn transaction, by which David should hereafter be restrained from personal exposure in battle.”

Henry, the puritan-evangelical, turns the soldiers’ care into a principle: “the lives of those who are as valuable to their country as David was ought to be preserved with a double care, both by themselves and others.” He notes the men had easily kept David from the field against Absalom (2 Sam. 18:3), but only this narrow escape could restrain him from the Philistines. The witnesses land together on the tenderness of the scene: a king rescued by a subject’s courage, and a people so jealous for their shepherd that they will not risk his light going out—a light that shines, Ellicott and Clarke agree, only because “the LORD lightened his darkness” (2 Sam. 22:29).
18:
The second giant falls at Gob, where “Sibbechai the Hushathite slew Saph.” The witnesses spend the verse on names and places, for the parallel in Chronicles differs at several points. On the site, Keil and Delitzsch note that “the scene of conflict is called Gob in our text, and Gezer in the Chronicles” (1 Chron. 20:4); against Thenius’s guess that “Gob” is a slip for “Gezer,” they object that “Gob occurs again in v.19,” and suggest instead “a small place somewhere near to Gezer.” The grammatical-historical Ellicott adds that the Septuagint reads “Gath,” and reasonably remarks that “it is not at all remarkable that the names of many small places should be lost after the lapse of three thousand years.” The wesleyan-arminian Clarke reports the manuscript variant “Nob” for “Gob” in “about forty of Kennicott’s and De Rossi’s MSS.”

On the men, both Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott identify Sibbechai as one of David’s mighty men (1 Chron. 11:29), “the leader of the eighth division of the army,” whose name appears as “Mebunnai the Hushathite” in 2 Sam. 23:27 “by a slight alteration in the letters.” The giant Saph is “written Sippai in the Chronicles,” where, Ellicott notes, “it is there said that the Philistines were subdued.” The reformed Gill simply sets the sequence—“after this … after the former battle.”

The clustering of textual notes is not idle to the witnesses; it is their way of honouring the passage as a genuine war-record, preserved in more than one archive with the small divergences that copying brings. Beneath the variants the fact stands firm: a second champion of Gath’s giant stock is cut down, this time not by the king but by one of his captains—the first instance of what Henry calls the pattern, that “the rest of the giants fell by the hand of David’s servants,” so that “God chooses by the weak things to confound the mighty” (1 Cor. 1:27). The champions of Israel were “no bigger nor stronger than other men; yet thus, by divine assistance, they mastered one giant after another.”
19:
Here the witnesses meet the chapter’s hardest textual knot: the Hebrew says Elhanan “slew … Goliath the Gittite,” yet everyone knows David slew Goliath. All agree the difficulty is real and the resolution lies in Chronicles. The wesleyan-arminian Clarke calls it “a most manifest corruption of the text, or gross mistake of the transcriber,” and points to 1 Chron. 20:5, which reads that “Elhanan, the son of Jair, slew Lahmi, the brother of Goliath the Gittite.” He explains the corruption mechanically: the word oregim (“weavers”) “has slipped out of one line into the other,” and “Beth-hallachmi” (the Bethlehemite) is a corruption of “eth Lahmi”; restore these and “the reading will be the same as in Chronicles,” a solution he credits to Kennicott.

Ellicott, for the grammatical-historical school, agrees that “oregim (= weavers) has accidentally crept into the text from the line below,” that “Jair” and “Jaare” are the same “with a slight transposition of the letters,” and that “the brother of” and the name “Lahmi” are supplied from Chronicles—indeed the very word “Beth-lemite” may be “a corruption of ‘Lahmi the brother of.’” Keil and Delitzsch report the scholarly dispute more evenly: “the majority of the expositors … regard the text of the Chronicles as the true and original one, and the text before us as simply corrupt,” while Bertheau and Böttcher “maintain the opposite opinion.” Either way the sense demanded is that Elhanan slew, not Goliath himself, but Goliath’s giant brother, “whose spear was like a weaver’s beam.”

Ellicott preserves the curious alternative the ancients offered. There is “a Jewish tradition that this Elhanan was David himself,” embalmed in the Chaldee paraphrase: “David the son of Jesse, the weaver of veils for the sanctuary, who was of Bethlehem, slew Goliath the Gittite.” He thinks it “altogether probable” that this Elhanan is the same as “Elhanan the son of Dodo of Bethlehem,” one of the thirty-seven heroes of 2 Sam. 23:24. The reformed Gill only marks it “another battle with them in the same place.” The witnesses close ranks on the point that matters: Scripture does not contradict itself here; a copyist’s eye slipped, Chronicles preserves the truer line, and the slain giant is Goliath’s brother—David’s single victory over Gath’s champion still standing unrivalled at the head of these later feats.
20:
The next giant is a monster of “great stature” with “six fingers on each hand and six toes on each foot,” who “defied Israel” and was slain by “Jonathan the son of Shimeah, David’s brother.” The witnesses note first the curiosity of his form. Keil and Delitzsch, for the grammatical-historical school, treat the polydactyly soberly, citing Pliny on Roman sedigiti and observing that “this peculiarity is even hereditary in some families.” The wesleyan-arminian Clarke matches him with modern cases—the eldest son of the emperor of Java in 1648, two families near Berlin, and “a young girl in the county of Londonderry, in Ireland, who had six fingers on each hand, and six toes on each foot,” whose “stature had nothing gigantic in it.” Keil and Delitzsch also parse the giant’s title, reading the archaic form as “a man of measures, or extensions,” where Chronicles has simply “a man of measure.”

But this verse is chiefly the occasion for Clarke’s great outburst of textual skepticism, the fullest in the chapter. Reviewing the whole passage he declares that “the text has suffered so materially as to distort, if not misrepresent, the principal facts,” and lists a dozen objections: the three-year famine unrecorded elsewhere; Saul’s massacre of the Gibeonites mentioned nowhere else; the improbability that God would “leave the choice of the atonement” to a heathen people or “require seven innocent men” to hang for their father; the Michal-for-Merab error of v.8; the hanging of bodies “against an express law of God” (Deut. 21:23); and the corrupted killing of Goliath in v.19. He concludes that “this part of the Jewish records has suffered much from rabbinical glosses, alterations, and additions,” the historical books having been “less carefully preserved” than the Law and the Prophets—yet even he ends by confessing that “the great foundation of God standeth sure.”

Here the witnesses stand furthest apart. Where Ellicott and Wesley laboured to justify the transaction and Keil and Delitzsch to explain its details, Clarke, on this verse, lets loose every doubt at once, treating much of the chapter as a scribal ruin. The reformed Gill takes the opposite tack, harmonizing quietly: this was “another battle,” and the giant one more of “the race of the giants.” The verse itself, past the controversy, records only the arithmetic of defiance and defeat: a taunting monster of twenty-four digits, and a nephew of David who silenced him. For all Clarke’s misgivings about the text, the witnesses who follow the narrative as it stands see in the six-fingered giant’s fall one more proof that Gath’s proud stock, however monstrous, could not stand before the servants of David’s God.
21:
The defiant giant is dispatched and the section sealed, and the witnesses attend to the slayer, the summary’s grammar, and its theology. On the man, the grammatical-historical Ellicott identifies Jonathan as “the nephew of David” (1 Chron. 20:7), “either the wily Jonadab mentioned in 2 Sam. 13:3, or, more probably, his brother,” and remarks that “David’s family connections seem to have constituted a clan of heroes.” The reformed Gill only notes that the giant “defied Israel … as Goliath had done some years ago.”

Keil and Delitzsch treat the closing verse (v.22) as a postscript summing up the whole, and explain its slightly irregular Hebrew—the accusative “these four” arising “from a species of attraction,” as though the writer still had the verb “slew” in mind: “as for these four, they were born to Rapha,” descendants of “the Rephaite family at Gath, where remnants of the aboriginal Canaanitish tribes of gigantic stature were still to be found” (Josh. 11:22). Crucially, Keil and Delitzsch insist the phrase “by the hand of David” is no mere formula: it “refers to the fact that David had personally fought with Ishbi-benob” in v.16—so the credit belongs both to the king’s own arm and to his servants’.

The witnesses read the tally as a fitting close to David’s martial life. Henry draws the arc: David “began his glory with the conquest of one giant, and here concludes it with the conquest of four,” and the lesson is that “it is folly for the strong man to glory in his strength,” for “God chooses by the weak things to confound the mighty.” The four sons of the giant, long the terror of Israel, “though long feared, fell at last.” So the verse gathers the four victories into one confession—that Gath’s giants fell not by human bulk but by the hand of David and, through his servants, by the hand of David’s God. What the young shepherd began against one Philistine champion in the valley of Elah, the aged king and his men complete against the last of the breed; and the same providence that once guided a stone now nerves the arms of his servants. The terror that had defied Israel through a generation is spent, and Gath’s house of giants is emptied by the hand of a faith that never trusted in its own strength.
22:
The closing verse repeats and rounds off the summary—“these four were born to the giant in Gath”—and the witnesses use it to trace the giants’ ancestry and to gather the chapter’s meaning. The reformed Gill is careful about the word “giant”: these four were born “not to Goliath, for one of them was his brother, but to some giant … of that place, for which it was famous.” He reports the Septuagint’s phrase that they were “the offspring of the giants in Gath, whose family was Repha,” and passes on the rabbinic fancy, from Abarbinel, that “Repha” was “a woman of the daughters of the giants,” whom “the Talmudists make the same with Orpah.” Above all Gill fixes their pedigree: “these giants, it is highly probable, were the descendants of the Anakim which remained in Gath after they were cut off by Joshua in other places”—the last survivors of the race whose remnant Joshua had left only in Gaza, Gath, and Ashdod (Josh. 11:22).

Ellicott, for the grammatical-historical school, adds a small precision: “they were all descendants of Rapha, but not necessarily all the sons of one man.” The four need not be literal brothers; they are of one gigantic stock, the Rephaim of Gath, the aboriginal terror whose few remaining families Israel had long dreaded.

With this the chapter ends, and the witnesses let its two halves answer each other. The first half buried a famine’s guilt in a family grave; the second buries the last giants of Gath in the dust of their defiance. Henry’s summary carries the pastoral weight the bare genealogy invites: “the most powerful enemies are often reserved for the last conflict,” and “death is a Christian’s last enemy, and a son of Anak; but, through him that triumphed for us, we hope to be more than conquerors at last, even over that enemy” (Rom. 8:37). So the record of David’s giants closes not in a boast of strength but in a promise: the Anakim who terrified Israel for generations fall one by one, and the last enemy too shall fall before the Son of David.
